Call 2025 - Round 3
Mobility of young people (KA152-YOU)
Form ID KA152-YOU-3A3C16C5 Deadline (Brussels Time) 01 Oct 2025 12:00:00
Project Summary
Please provide short answers to the following questions to summarise the information you have provided in the rest of the
application form.
Please use full sentences and clear language. In case your project is accepted, the summary you provided will be made
public by the European Commission and the National Agencies.
What do you want to achieve by implementing the project? What are the objectives of your project? Please specify from the
perspective of youth work practice.
The project aims to empower Generation Z by helping young people develop personal growth, resilience and emotional well-
being through transformative international experiences. By implementing this project, we want to create a space where
young people can face the global mental health challenges together and find solutions through teamwork and collaboration.
Mental health issues such as depression, anxiety and societal pressure are not limited to one country or region, but are
global concerns, especially for Gen Z. Through this project, we aim to raise awareness, encourage open conversations and
provide practical tools to deal with these issues. Our main goal is to create a generation that is more open to asking for help,
supporting each other and finding solutions for mental health challenges. We want participants to understand that mental
health issues are global and it's important to talk about them openly. By creating an environment where participants feel safe
to express their feelings, we aim to reduce the stigma around mental health. The project will focus on helping participants
build resilience and improve their emotional well-being through group activities, sports and creative engagement. By facing
challenges together, participants will gain the tools to manage stress and anxiety more effectively. By bringing together
young people from different countries, we aim to show the importance of working together to find solutions for common
problems. Participants will learn how to cooperate with people from different backgrounds and develop skills for
international collaboration, which is crucial in addressing global issues like mental health. We want participants to explore
their own strengths, hobbies and interests and understand how these can contribute to their mental well-being. The project
will offer activities that allow them to discover new passions and ways to relax, helping them grow both personally and
professionally. Through shared experiences and challenges, participants will form strong bonds with each other, which will
help create a supportive network. This community will be able to continue offering emotional support and share ideas even
after the project ends, extending the impact beyond the project’s duration. To achieve these objectives, we will use various
non-formal education methods that encourage participation, creativity and self-reflection. Here are some of the key
methodologies we plan to use: Interactive workshops will allow participants to actively engage with topics related to mental
health and personal development. For example, in the "Let's Talk About It" session, participants will engage in speed-dating
style conversations on mental health topics. This will help them open up and share their thoughts in a safe and supportive
environment. After key activities, participants will gather in small groups to reflect on their experiences. These group
discussions will help them process what they have learned and allow them to support each other by sharing insights and
personal stories. We will organize activities such as group challenges and city treasure hunts, where participants must work
together to complete tasks. These projects will build teamwork skills, encourage creative problem-solving and help
participants understand the importance of collaboration. Games, sports and creative tasks like art or theatre will be used to
promote mental well-being in a playful and engaging way. In the "A Sound Mind in a Sound Body" activity, each country will
present a game or sport from their culture, which will then be discussed in terms of its impact on mental health. These
activities will help participants connect both mentally and physically, promoting holistic development. Through events like
Intercultural Night, participants will present their cultures through food, drink and performances. This will build mutual
respect and appreciation for different cultures, as well as highlight the shared nature of mental health challenges. Each
participant will have opportunities for self-exploration, especially on the day dedicated to mental well-being and self- care.
This will allow them to reflect on their mental health journey, identify their strengths and set or track personal goals for
growth. To ensure the success of the project and measure its impact, we will use several evaluation methods: At the end of
each day, participants will be asked to provide feedback through short surveys or reflection sessions. This will help us
understand how the activities affected their mental well-being and personal growth. After key activities, group discussions
will allow us to gauge how participants are processing what they have learned and whether they feel more comfortable
discussing mental health topics. We will assess specific outcomes like increased mental health awareness, improvement in
teamwork skills and personal development progress.
What activities do you plan to implement? What is the number and profile of the participants involved?
We plan to implement a wide variety of activities that will help the participants grow, learn and connect with each other, all
while focusing on mental health and personal development. The group will consist of 35 participants, aged between 18 and
25, including 5 team leaders, aged minimum 18. Participants will come from five different countries, creating a diverse and
multicultural environment. The activities will be guided by two experienced facilitators from Hungary, who are skilled in
mental health and youth engagement. Additionally, a mental health expert with experience working with young people will
join for specific sessions that require deeper knowledge. Our activities are designed to create a safe, welcoming and
engaging environment for everyone involved. We will start with ice-breaking games, where participants can get to know
each other through fun and interactive activities. These ice-breaker and team building activities will be held both indoors and
outdoors, depending on the weather. We will ensure that all participants feel included from the start. One of the core
activities we plan is called "Let's talk about it." This will be an open discussion where participants will engage in rapid date-

like conversations on mental health topics. After these quick discussions, we will encourage participants to reflect on how
they felt talking openly about their thoughts and emotions. This session will create a free space for participants to express
themselves, with facilitators offering support but allowing participants to lead the discussion. Based on previous
experiences, we expect this session to continue into the night, as participants find it important to connect on a deeper level.
"A Sound Mind in a Sound Body" is another key activity. In this session, each of the five participating countries will present
and organize a traditional game or sport from their home country. This will include childhood games or national sports that
participants grew up playing. After playing these games, we will discuss how such activities impact mental health, both in
youth and adulthood. This session highlights the connection between physical activity, fun and mental well-being.
Participants will have the chance to reflect on the positive emotions these games bring back and how they can use them to
relieve stress in their lives today. Intercultural Night will be a cultural exchange event, where national teams will present their
country’s food, drinks, dances and traditions. This is an important bonding activity, as it encourages participants to
appreciate and celebrate the diversity in the group. By sharing their cultures, participants will feel proud of their
backgrounds and learn to respect and enjoy others’ traditions. It also aligns with our goal of promoting international
collaboration and understanding. We will also conduct a city tour and treasure hunt in Pécs. During this tour, participants will
have small challenges, such as taking creative photos, connecting with local citizens and asking them about their methods
for managing stress. This activity not only helps participants explore the city but also deepens their understanding of mental
health in different contexts. It also provides an opportunity to engage with the local community and practice teamwork. If the
weather does not cooperate, we are prepared to adjust the schedule to ensure everyone can enjoy the full experience. We
will also incorporate other non-formal learning methods, such as open debates, theatre performances and group tasks. Each
day of the project will have a specific theme. For example: -Day 1 will focus on introducing the project and Erasmus+. -Day 2
will address understanding the current mental health situation with the theme "Where are we now?" -Day 3 will explore the
role of sports and games in overcoming mental health challenges under the theme "Playful and Mindful." -Day 4 will be a mix
of free time and group tasks in the city, with the theme "Exploration." -Day 5 will focus on mental health awareness, learning
how to care for mental well-being, with the theme "Mental Well- being." -Day 6 will encourage self-exploration and self-care.
-Day 7 will be about reflecting on the week’s activities, summarizing the learnings and saying goodbye with a final closing
event. These activities are designed to contribute to the overall objectives of the project: empowering young people, building
personal growth and raising awareness about mental health. The discussions, games and workshops will help participants
build resilience and learn new strategies for mental wellness. By engaging in physical and mental activities, they will develop
problem-solving skills, improve emotional well-being and appreciate the importance of teamwork and international
collaboration. To measure the effectiveness of our activities, we will use several methods: Participant feedback, group
discussions and measurable outcomes.
What results and impact do you expect your project to have?
The project aims to empower Generation Z by providing them with opportunities for personal growth, resilience-building and
emotional well-being. We expect that the results of our project will lead to significant positive changes in participants and
their communities. One of the main results we anticipate is improved mental health awareness and support among
participants. By addressing mental health challenges openly, we hope that participants will feel more comfortable discussing
their feelings and seeking help when needed. This openness will likely lead to a more supportive community atmosphere.
Participants will learn that mental health is a shared concern and by recognizing this, they can engage in conversations with
their peers and family members about mental health issues. This could encourage more young people to seek support and
share their experiences, ultimately leading to a healthier community. In the long term, we expect our participants to apply the
skills and knowledge gained during the project in their daily lives. They will likely develop a stronger sense of self-
awareness, enabling them to understand their emotions and reactions better. This self-awareness can help them manage
stress, anxiety and other challenges more effectively. Additionally, the resilience-building techniques they learn will equip
them to face future obstacles with confidence. As participants become more resilient, they will be better prepared to support
their peers and family members, creating a ripple effect of mental wellness throughout their communities. We foresee a
lasting impact on the communities where these young people live. As participants share their experiences and newfound
skills with their friends and families, they will contribute to a culture of mental health awareness and support. This cultural
shift can encourage community members to prioritize mental well-being and seek resources for support. By creating
teamwork and collaboration during the project, participants will be better equipped to work with others to address
community issues, promoting a sense of shared responsibility and collaboration. The project will also actively engage local
communities beyond the participants. We plan to organize events and initiatives aimed at sharing insights and learnings with
local youth and organizations. For example, we will host workshops or seminars in local schools or community centers,
where participants can present their experiences and share techniques for emotional well-being. This outreach will create
opportunities for young people in the community to engage in discussions about mental health and personal development,
extending the project's impact beyond those who directly participated. We aim to establish collaborations with local
organizations, such as mental health services, youth centers and educational institutions. These partnerships will help
amplify the project’s impact within the community and beyond. Local organizations can provide resources, support and
expertise, helping to create a sustainable framework for ongoing mental health initiatives. Together, we can work on creating
programs that encourage young people to continue exploring their well-being and personal development. By building these
connections, we hope to create a network of support that extends beyond the project itself. This collaboration will ensure
that participants have access to continued resources and support as they navigate their mental health journeys. We also
plan to create a platform, such as a website or social media group, where participants and community members can share

participants to remain connected and involved in the conversation around mental health. We expect the project to have
significant results and a lasting impact on participants and their communities. By empowering young people through
personal growth and emotional wellness, we aim to create a culture of mental health awareness and support. The skills and
insights gained during the project will not only benefit participants personally but also create a ripple effect, positively

have transformed our lives through these projects, developing key competencies, soft skills, and forming connections with
friends across Europe, among many other benefits. We are dedicated to delivering youth work at a quality we would expect
from ourselves, believing that the best way to engage in youth work is through young people working with young people. We
are truly an Erasmus+ generation at Square of Youth Association.
Our mission is to connect Hungarian youth with Europe. We aim to provide opportunities for them to explore Europe,
connect with their European peers, and empower them to become active citizens. We encourage them to take initiatives and
contribute to their communities.
We also aim and working to:
Help young people learn through non-formal learning activities and enhance their soft skills.
Increasing youth participation through our projects and providing international opportunities for young people.
Connect youth with stakeholders and decision-makers, facilitating the translation of youth voices to them and increasing
youth involvement.
Promote dialogue among young people from EU member states and candidate countries, which includes collaborating with
partners from outside the EU as well.
Internationality is a key component of our activities because we believe that international opportunities and mobilities have
the potential to improve young people's learning and growth on a high level.
We primarily focus on three key topics, but due to our diversity and ambition, we are open to many other areas as well. The
three key topics are:
Entrepreneurship and empowering young people to become entrepreneurs. Several of our members became entrepreneurs
at a young age. We believe entrepreneurship not only as a business endeavor but also for its entrepreneurial skills, which
are great soft skills in many aspects of one’s life.
Active citizenship and youth empowerment. Aiming for our projects and activities can have a multiplier effect and create
impact on a broader scale.
Topics closely connected to the European Union and being a European citizen.
SOYA is a member of the Danube Youth Organization Network and is committed to fostering cooperation among youth-
focused organizations in the Danube Region. We find this particularly important in facilitating meetings with decision-makers
and establishing cross-border.
What are the activities and experience of the organisation in youth work? Please provide information on your organisation’s
/ group’s regular youth work activities.
Square of Youth Association (SOYA) is a youth-led organisation where all activities are designed, planned, and
implemented by young people. Our work focuses on youth entrepreneurship, inclusion, sustainability, and active
participation in international mobility programs, providing young people with opportunities to develop skills, broaden their
perspectives, and take part in meaningful learning experiences.
Regarding entrepreneurship, SOYA implemented the "Entrepreneur in a Week" Erasmus+ Youth Exchange in April 2025.
Participants gained essential skills and knowledge to develop entrepreneurial ideas, including business plan writing,
pitching, and financing. The project encouraged young people to explore their potential and take initiative in their personal
and professional growth.
We are also implemented a Visegrad V4 Gen Mini-Grant project: Policy-Making Hackathon for Young Entrepreneurs, which
took place in Budapest from March 5 to 9, 2025. This initiative brought together young people for a 5-day policy-making
hackathon, where they analyzed the barriers to entrepreneurship and propose solutions. The outcomes of this project
compiled into a policy document and presented to decision-makers and stakeholders, ensuring that young voices contribute
to shaping policies that support youth entrepreneurship.
SOYA is strongly committed to inclusion and intercultural exchange. Our ESC-30 Solidarity project, "Board Games for
Inclusion", focuses on bringing together Hungarian and international students in Budapest through board games. This
initiative creates an informal space where young people from different backgrounds can meet and play together. The project
started in September 2024 and became a regular local activity of SOYA in Budapest, helping foreign young people settle
into their new surroundings while providing local students with opportunities to engage in intercultural experiences.
We also play an active role in democratizing access to Erasmus+ opportunities. Many young people are unaware of the
different mobility programs available to them, so we organize Erasmus+ information sessions that introduce them to various
possibilities, including Erasmus for Young Entrepreneurs and the European Solidarity Corps (ESC). By providing clear and
accessible guidance, we aim to ensure that young people from all backgrounds can take advantage of these opportunities.
As part of our outreach efforts, SOYA exhibited at Sziget Festival 2024, engaging directly with festival-goers to promote
Erasmus+ through interactive and engaging activities. Our presence at the festival allowed us to connect with a broad
international audience, answer questions about mobility programs, and encourage young people to explore new
opportunities. Moving forward, we aim to expand our presence at festivals and public events, ensuring that Erasmus+
reaches even more young people in informal, engaging settings.
In addition to our structured projects, all SOYA members have extensive experience in Erasmus+ programs, having
participated in youth exchanges, training courses, university Erasmus+ semesters, and ESC volunteering projects. Some of
our team members have also been involved in Erasmus for Young Entrepreneurs, while others have designed and facilitated
non-formal learning activities in international settings. Our deep personal engagement in these initiatives has shaped our
approach, ensuring that our projects are youth-centered, impactful, and directly relevant to the needs of young people today.

Background and experience
Please briefly present the organisation / the group
Established as an official NGO in 2023, Colony of Creators began its journey as a group of dedicated young individuals in
2017. Through persistence and perseverance, our mission is to expand and offer more opportunities to the youth. Colony of
Creators is a non-profit, non-governmental organization that advances knowledge, entrepreneurship, civic society
development, non-formal education, and innovative learning techniques, primarily targeting the youth (15 to 35). We uphold
values of tolerance, democracy, diversity, voluntariness, and openness. Our objective is to stimulate local community
development via collaborations across private, public, and civil society sectors. We advocate for inclusivity in non-formal
education and foster a spirit of innovation and entrepreneurship. We are driven by the determination to create a meaningful
impact and provide an expanded array of opportunities for young people. In addition to serving people with disabilities, our
broad scope of operations covers areas such as human rights, anti-trafficking, reproductive health, gender equality, sports,
media production, event organization, technology, humanitarian actions, criminality and corruption prevention, start-ups,
project management, refugee support, and publishing. Our key goals are safeguarding the environment, fostering youth
engagement at a global scale, and creating a conducive work environment. We undertake initiatives such as conducting
research on gamified activities, disseminating information, collaborating with international associations, and encouraging
collaborations for environmental protection. Our focus areas include: Youth Empowerment: Prioritizing equal opportunities,
supporting potential realization, and including youth issues in policies. Education and Training: Providing affordable, quality,
and skill-oriented education with guidance in soft skills and gender-violence prevention. Youth and Non-Formal Education:
Improving information access, enhancing media sector employment, combating fake news, working with marginalized
groups, and promoting gender equality. Employment Creation: Promoting youth employment, aiming for a 10% annual
reduction in youth unemployment, and facilitating youth engagement in economic growth sectors. Health: Promoting
responsible sexual behavior, promoting mental health, and raising awareness about healthy lifestyles and reproductive
health. Environment: Encouraging youth engagement in environmental protection, innovation in environmental management,
and eco-friendly behavior. Arts and Culture: Promoting and protecting local arts and culture and supporting various forms of
artistic expression. Sport and Recreation: Ensuring access to sports facilities, promoting sports as a tool for social
responsibility, and using sports for inclusion and education. Our organization also advocates for democracy, European
standards, tolerance, social solidarity, environmental safety, gender equality, human rights, and socially responsible
entrepreneurship. We cooperate with domestic and international organizations and facilitate sustainable economic
development projects. Our vision is to be the leading organization in Madeira and Portugal by fostering young leadership,

Project Rationale
In this section, you are asked to explain the aims of your project, what you want to achieve and what are its expected
benefits on participants and participating organisations. Make sure to read the questions carefully and address all sub-
questions.
Needs and Objectives
Why do you want to carry out this project? Please describe the issues and needs you want to address and your project’s
objectives.
We decided to work on a serious topic that we feel really connected with as most of our team members are also part of Gen
Z (generation Z). We were struggling also with similar issues as the younger Gen Z youth. No time for hobbies, improper
habits and the extreme level of rush every single day. We managed to find solutions for many of these challenges that help
us to grow in a healthy way. That’s why previously, we asked around 25 people from the same generation if they know how
to overcome the challenges that are influencing them. We got many more interests while being on SZIGET festival, when we
could meet with young people directly and engage with them deeper. Their responses were a desire to understand it better
and learn about them, especially solutions to stay in focus and avoid depression. Hence, we started to work on this topic and
project with them together. For (Gen Z) it’s more challenging than others would think as first COVID-19 made their teenage
years miserable, now the continuous inflation and the war in Ukraine or in the Middle East. All of these are connected to
each other mentally for Gen Z and this is just the tip of the iceberg. In the European Union people are living in good
conditions generally, still they are more depressed than people at the same age in other continents, this is why we would like
to explore this topic deeper. Furthermore, the other main motivation behind creating this project is to address the specific
challenges and needs faced by Gen Z. We want to carry out this project because we feel that the youth of this time are
undergoing a lot of stress. Gen Z is grappling with various issues, caused by excessive use of the internet and by other
challenges like pressure from society and the lack of physical activity. Depression has become increasingly prevalent
among Gen Z and societal pressures often exacerbate this condition. Excessive screen time is another pressing concern for
Gen Z. The pandemic has had significant effects on Gen Z, including disrupted education, limited social interactions and
increased stress levels. Additionally, the lack of physical activity and the diminishing importance of play in the lives of Gen Z
pose significant concerns for their holistic development. Through this project, we want to emphasize the importance of
mindful engagement. Our project acknowledges these challenges and provides a supportive environment where
participants can reflect, heal and build resilience through meaningful international experiences. We would like to see the
participants to explore the topic from various perspectives. Once they are there and they start to share their feelings,
problems or just thoughts related to them, they will also start to work on them. This will help to talk about what is really
connected with the stress factors that they are struggling with as a generation. Ultimately, the objective of "Self exploration
in the jungle of life" is to empower Gen Z by combining elements of mental health support, mindful engagement and physical
activity. We also make them aware of how they can proactively utilize new methods to combat these negative effects.
How does your project link to the objectives of the Erasmus programme and those of Youth Exchanges?
Our project is closely connected to the objectives of the Erasmus program, particularly within the framework of Youth
Exchanges. It aligns with Erasmus+ priorities by promoting social inclusion, intercultural learning, personal development and
European values, all while addressing the critical issue of mental health among Gen Z. Through this international youth
exchange, we aim to empower young participants by giving them opportunities to improve their skills, gain cultural
understanding and develop solutions for the mental health challenges they face in today’s world. The Erasmus+ program
emphasizes creating youth engagement, personal development and the acquisition of key competencies. Our project does
exactly this by offering non-formal learning opportunities in a supportive and collaborative environment. One of the key goals
of the Erasmus program is to enhance young people's awareness of their place in a larger European community and our
project seeks to do this by bringing together participants from several European countries. Through a series of activities and
workshops, they will explore different approaches to mental health, develop their communication and collaboration skills and
become more open-minded and inclusive. Intercultural learning is a central component of our project. By bringing together
participants from different countries, we create a platform for them to experience cultural exchange in a practical way.
Activities such as the Intercultural Night will allow participants to present their countries, cultures, food and traditions. This
interaction encourages mutual respect and appreciation for diversity, which are key values of the Erasmus program.
Participants will leave the exchange with a deeper understanding of how mental health challenges vary across cultures, but
also with a shared sense of responsibility to address them collectively as Europeans. During the project, participants will
also gain key competencies, particularly in intercultural communication, teamwork and problem-solving. By engaging in
discussions, group activities and workshops, they will develop skills that are essential in today’s globalized world, such as
how to collaborate with people from different cultural backgrounds and how to find common solutions to shared problems.
These competencies are highly valued by the Erasmus+ program and contribute directly to enhancing participants'
employability and social inclusion. We recognize that mental health challenges often disproportionately affect young people
with fewer opportunities or those who face social or economic disadvantages.We aim to ensure that all participants, feel
included and supported. We will use strategies such as group work, individual support and tailored activities to ensure that
everyone can engage in the activities fully. During the ice-breaking sessions and group workshops, facilitators will pay
special attention to those who might feel less confident or hesitant to participate. By creating an environment where every

communities. By raising awareness of the different ways mental health issues can affect young people, we hope to equip
participants with the knowledge and tools they need to support their peers back home. This focus on mental health as a

young people in Europe. Through the activities in our project, participants will develop key competencies that are important
for their personal and professional development. For example, they will improve their problem-solving and creativity through
workshops that require them to work in teams and come up with innovative solutions to mental health challenges. Their
digital skills will also be enhanced, especially through the city treasure hunt and other tasks that involve using digital tools
and cameras to connect with local people and solve challenges. Participants will also gain emotional intelligence, which is
increasingly recognized as an important skill for both personal well-being and professional success. By working through
group challenges and reflecting on their own mental health, participants will develop greater self-awareness, empathy and
resilience. These are important competencies that align with Erasmus+ goals of preparing young people for a rapidly
changing world. The success of the project will be measured through participant feedback, self-assessment and group
reflections. Each participant will receive a Youthpass certificate, which will highlight the competencies they have developed
during the exchange. The feedback we collect will allow us to measure how well participants have improved their
understanding of mental health.
Impact
How will your project benefit the young participants involved in the project, during and after the project lifetime?
Our project will offer great benefits to the young participants, both during and after its completion, helping them to develop
important skills and gain valuable experiences. These benefits align with the Erasmus+ program goals, which focus on
preparing young people for their future and improving their personal and professional skills. The participants will have the
opportunity to grow through various activities, focusing on mental health, teamwork, leadership, problem-solving and
intercultural understanding. During the project, the participants will learn basic knowledge about mental health care and
mental well-being. They will discuss the challenges that Gen Z faces, such as stress, societal pressure and fears about the
future. Participants will also learn about different methods and techniques for managing these issues, which they can later
apply to their own lives and share with others. The project will include various non-formal education methods like interactive
workshops, group discussions and creative activities. These activities will allow participants to practice teamwork, critical
thinking and problem-solving. They will work together to discuss mental health topics and come up with solutions. For
example, participants will be given group tasks where they must work together to create strategies for improving mental
well-being. These experiences will help them understand how to collaborate and develop creativity. A key element of the
project is the use of the Youthpass, which will help participants to reflect on their learning and personal development. By
using this tool, they will learn how to assess their progress and identify the skills they have gained. This will boost their
confidence and motivate them to continue improving after the project. The project will also create a supportive and safe
environment where participants can try new things and express themselves freely. They will be encouraged to share their
thoughts and ideas and no one will be judged for making mistakes. This will help build their resilience and self-confidence,
as they will learn how to overcome challenges without fear of failure. After the project ends, the participants will have a

The impact of our project will extend far beyond the direct participants and partner organizations, reaching local, regional,
national, and European levels. On a local level, the project will serve as an advocate for nurturing a more inclusive and
supportive environment for Generation Z individuals, addressing their fears, challenges, and mental health concerns.

community, enhancing local tourism and economic activity in Harkány and Pécs. The interactions between local youth and
international participants will create an opportunity for knowledge-sharing and mutual learning, allowing young people in the
region to broaden their perspectives and gain insights into different cultural approaches to mental well-being. Additionally,
our collaboration with local NGOs and youth organizations will further amplify the impact, ensuring that the discussions
initiated during the project continue within the local youth sector. At the regional and national level, the project's influence will
be strengthened by our active online presence and dissemination strategy. By sharing key findings, experiences, and best
practices on social media platforms and through our website, we will reach young people across Hungary who might not
have had the opportunity to participate directly in the exchange. This will help raise awareness about mental health and well-
being among Hungarian youth, providing them with access to valuable resources and tools that can support their personal
development. Our strong connections with other Hungarian NGOs, such as 21 Nap and ICRP, will enable us to collaborate
on spreading the project's message and promoting its outcomes nationwide. Through this network, we aim to encourage
discussions on youth mental health, resilience, and self-exploration beyond our immediate participants, influencing a
broader audience of young people, educators, and professionals. On a European level, the project's impact will be
magnified by our commitment to translating all project outputs into English, making them accessible to a wider audience.
The research, best practices, and materials developed during and after the project will be shared with our international
partners and beyond. We plan to implement a long-term research initiative with our partner organizations, ensuring that the
knowledge and insights gained from the project contribute to an ongoing discussion at the European level. Through an
informative campaign, we will present key findings from our research, engaging youth workers, mental health professionals,
and young people themselves in discussions about mental well-being, emotional intelligence, and self-development. This
will create opportunities for cross-border collaboration and knowledge- sharing, strengthening the European youth sector’s
approach to mental health support. By making our best practices available to other organizations, including those with less
experience in Erasmus+ projects, we aim to create a more mindful and inclusive NGO sector. The project's methodology
and findings will be structured in a way that allows other organizations to implement similar initiatives, thereby increasing the
project’s long-term impact across Europe. This approach will contribute to strengthening European identity and cohesion,
reinforcing the idea that youth mental health is a shared challenge that requires collective solutions. Through these efforts,
"Self Exploration in the Jungle of Life" will not only support the personal growth of its participants but also inspire broader
systemic changes in how youth mental health is addressed across different levels of society.

Project Design
Preparation, support and follow-up
How will you prepare the participants before the start of the activity (e.g. intercultural, linguistic, risk-prevention etc.) and
how will you support them during and after the activity?
The selection of the participants will happen based on several criterias such as level of English, interests of topic, active help
with preparation or age, (gender will be considered for gender balance). The participants will be able to fill out an online
form, where they can see the infopack of the project and also they can answer the application related questions. Apart from
the above: experience, interested role also, we will ask for consent related to GDPR rules as well as for taking videos and
pictures. After the closing of the deadline we will consider all applications and based on the above criterias we will select the
team and then we will inform everyone accordingly. After the selection of the participants, we will hold a meeting for
everyone to share the “must know” information about the project before their arrival. This meeting will take place online and
it will support logistical questions, as well as venue and topic related information sharing. This information will be also
included in the infopack that will be ready before the applicants start applying, but the online meeting will be the one, where
they can ask their specific questions or where we can share further information and updates with the participants. We will
also require from the partner organisations to make a meeting with their own national teams and discuss the rules of their
team, select a team leader, introduce the youth exchange and introduce the topic. We will do the same with our participants.
Through this activity we will get to know each other a bit more and we can discuss questions related to the trip to the venue.
This meeting will cover the preparation of the participants. They will be able to discuss the culture night elements, their
specific ideas and contribution to the sessions and their connection points to the topic by introducing their knowledge about
it. They will prepare about the discussed subtopics as well, so they can be active during the sessions, also important to
mention a kind of homework related to the most common Gen Z challenges by asking their group of friends as well as by
doing a small research from national statistics. The responsible team leaders will also help for the teams as well as the local
NGO representatives. To create and maintain a great national and international atmosphere with the participants they will be
able to get to know each other earlier on the Facebook group, by posting their imaginative stories with their mugs, also they
will be at the same Whatsapp group, so for the travelling days they can discuss other activities together. Linguistically, the
participants should reach at least a basic level of English so they can be active at the activities, but we are expecting
different levels of English in speaking or overall in our national team as well as from the partners. The high level English
speakers, also the team leaders will be encouraged to help anytime for their fellow participants. The risk-prevention will be
communicated at the introduction of the project by introducing the rules, also they will sign a “participant’s pledge” where
they will agree on terms and conditions related to their own safety as well. Such as bullying, skipping sessions, drugs and
over drinking is not tolerated. Medical interventions will be taken as well, physical and mental too e.g. enough free time, well
balanced theory and practice among non-formal educational methods and hand sanitizers in the session- or bathroom. Also,
we will pay attention to the participants’ allergies if they have any. There will be more varieties available, also two coffee
breaks will be provided for refreshment between sessions. There will be one day when we will leave the conference room
and we will dedicate it to the venue where we are with some city challenges, for this we will also be aware of the food by
preparing packages and get all the necessities that are needed such as tickets, materials for the city challenge. After the
project, we will provide all the support for giving the reimbursement to the participants as fast as possible. We will help their
understanding by the outcome that we prepare from the learning materials created at the project. They will get the support
for the social media posts from their side as well as from the NGO’s side, also the help for their dissemination activity. After
the project’s dissemination is over, we will continue the work on the subtopics that we choose with the partners who are
willing to participate in those projects as well. Then we will reach out to the participants for long-term research purposes.
These participants can help in their own way by filling out a questionnaire or being part of a research. We will share those
results on our website and social media, hence we will reach youth workers and other Gen Z members, who can read about
the topic more and ask for help, so we can assist to make their issues less stressful by listening and supporting them.
What measures will you put in place to ensure the safety and protection of participants?
To ensure the safety and protection of participants during the project, we will implement a comprehensive set of measures
that address both physical and mental well-being. These measures will not only focus on the immediate safety of
participants but will also contribute to the long-term sustainability of a safe and supportive community environment. Before
the project begins, we will conduct a thorough risk assessment to identify potential hazards and implement strategies to
mitigate them. The accommodation provider will be carefully vetted to ensure compliance with all safety regulations. Fire
safety protocols will be reviewed, and first aid kits will be readily available. Hand sanitizers and soap will be provided in all
common areas to maintain good hygiene. Seating arrangements during sessions will be designed to provide personal space
and comfort, contributing to both physical health and mental relaxation. During outdoor activities such as city tours or
community engagement initiatives, a designated member of the international group who speaks the local language will
always be present. This ensures smooth communication, helps prevent misunderstandings, and provides participants with
an extra layer of safety. Emergency phone numbers, including contacts for local hospitals, emergency services, and project
organizers, will be visibly displayed in the main session room and included in the participant information pack. All
participants will be required to have valid medical insurance, and we will maintain a record of nearby medical facilities to
provide immediate assistance if needed. Ensuring mental well-being is just as crucial as maintaining physical safety. The
project activities will be structured to promote a healthy balance between engagement and relaxation. Energizers will be
included at the start of each session to help participants feel active and engaged, while scheduled coffee breaks will allow

unnecessary stress or pressure on participants. Outdoor activities will be incorporated to provide mental relief, and safe
spaces will be established where participants can openly express their thoughts and emotions. Privacy and data protection
will also be prioritized. Participants' consent will be obtained before taking any photos or videos, in full compliance with
GDPR regulations. A “participant’s pledge” will outline the project’s rules, including the prohibition of drug use, the
requirement to have valid travel and health insurance, and expectations for respectful behavior. This document will also
inform participants about their rights regarding privacy and data security. The long-term sustainability of a safe and inclusive
community will be ensured through continuous monitoring and engagement with participants beyond the project duration.
One of our key objectives is to create a network of support that extends beyond the event itself. This will be facilitated
through an online platform where participants can share experiences, seek guidance, and stay connected with each other.
This platform will serve as a space for ongoing discussions on mental well-being and youth empowerment, reinforcing the
project's impact even after its completion. To ensure that the safety measures remain effective, we will establish a feedback
mechanism where participants can report any concerns in a confidential manner. A designated safeguarding officer will be
available to address any issues that arise and ensure that participants feel supported at all times. Additionally, a buddy
system will be introduced, pairing participants with peers who can offer mutual support and guidance throughout the
program. The community-level sustainability plan will focus on embedding the project's principles into the local and
international networks of participating organizations. By sharing best practices and training local youth workers on effective
risk management and mental health support strategies, we will contribute to the development of safer environments in future
projects. The dissemination of these measures will be facilitated through workshops, social media engagement, and follow-
up events, ensuring that the safety and well-being of young people remain a priority in youth mobility projects. We will also
prepare a "risk assessment plan". This will have not just a contact list in case of emergencies to the organizers but also to
the necessary authorities. We will introduce it already at the online APV, as well as on the first day of the exchange. This will
help us to overcome the possibilities of different scenarios that can be prevented. Such as fire, leaving the territory of the
camp and other not desired happenings. It will be made to be clear for everyone involved to whom to report or raise an
issue.
What activities are foreseen after the end of the Youth Exchange? How will the participants follow-up on the activity?
After the Youth Exchange, we have planned several activities to ensure that participants and partner organizations continue
to benefit from the project and share their knowledge with others. These activities aim to keep participants engaged,
encourage them to spread what they have learned and increase the project’s impact both locally and online. Immediately
after the Youth Exchange, we will work with our partner organizations to mobilize their national teams and organize
dissemination events in their home countries. These events will allow participants to share what they have learned during
the exchange with their communities. Our organization will also plan a similar event locally, with the help of participants from
our country. These dissemination events will focus on mental health challenges faced by Gen Z, promoting the solutions and
techniques discussed during the exchange. Participants will present their experiences, discuss the project’s topics and offer
insights into mental health and well-being. This will ensure that the project’s message reaches a wide audience beyond just
the participants. To increase the visibility of the project, we will encourage our partner organizations and participants to
share their experiences on social media. Our partners with us will post updates about the project and the topic of mental
health. On our side, we will share posts across our social media platforms to reach a broader audience. Participants will also
be encouraged to share their own experiences from the exchange on their personal social media accounts. Even one or two
posts per participant will help spread the project’s message and raise awareness about Gen Z mental health challenges.
Personal stories on social media can have a significant impact, especially when shared by young people themselves. We
plan to continue the project’s mission through our website (https://squareofyouth.eu), where we will post related content
such as research studies, news, best practices and mental health methods. This website will serve as a hub for youth
workers and young people who are interested in mental health challenges and solutions. Participants can contribute to this
by sharing their own stories, experiences or reflections on the project. Additionally, we plan to conduct a large research
project involving participants (if they are still interested) to explore Gen Z mental health challenges more deeply. This will
keep participants involved in meaningful ways, even after the exchange has ended. We will stay in touch with participants
through a Facebook group, WhatsApp chat and email. These platforms will be used to share information about future
activities, research opportunities and other resources. By keeping communication open, we aim to keep participants
engaged with the project and ensure they have the support they need to continue their journey of personal growth and
learning. Participants will also be encouraged to post on the project website, where they can share their experiences and
contribute to ongoing discussions about mental health. One of the key follow-up activities will be encouraging participants to
become ambassadors for the project and the topic of mental health in their local communities. We will ask participants to
organize small workshops, presentations or events in their hometowns or schools, where they can share the knowledge and
skills they gained during the Youth Exchange. This will not only help spread awareness of mental health challenges but also
allow participants to further develop their presentation and leadership skills. By becoming ambassadors, participants will
play an important role in extending the project’s impact and making a difference in their communities. We understand that
organizing dissemination activities can be challenging, so we will support our partners to ensure that their follow-up activities
are enjoyable and stress-free. We will provide to each other with ready-made materials, such as social media post
templates, presentation slides and detailed instructions for organizing our events. This support will help partners create
engaging and effective dissemination activities without feeling overwhelmed. Since the project focuses on reducing stress
and promoting well-being, we aim to ensure that the follow-up activities align with these values. After the Youth Exchange,
participants will engage in a variety of follow-up activities, including dissemination events, social media sharing, content

Recognition of learning outcomes
How will you support participants to be aware of what they have learned and which competences they have developed or
improved? Please remember to include the methods that support reflection and documentation of the learning outcomes in
the daily timetable of each activity.
The final outcome will be based on several steps that we introduce during the project, hence it’s also important to include as
their learning steps. We will create that outcome as a booklet, video, article and presentation with the help of the
participants. At the end of the “what did we learn?” session they will present this to each other, until then they will have time
to finalise their works. Then, we will summarize what we got from this project and what we will work on together more in the
future after the project. Open sharing, looking at the walls - where the prepared materials will hang – will be part of the
closing session where we will think back on what really happened through the whole week. During a specified session we
are planning to introduce the key competences at the beginning of the project (first day, second session). We will explore the
importance of Youthpass and self-evaluation by getting through the topic, also presenting together the key competences to
each other. After presenting this to each other they will have a deeper understanding about the topics of key competences
and why we are using it and what they can use it for. At the end of every day, after the last session but before dinner we will
make time for the daily evaluations - in connection with reflection time - that first they will document themselves alone and
then gather to small teams and talk about the happenings of the day, here they can share their happy and sad moments as
well as their insights and feedbacks, also they can propose their ideas to change something. There will be enough time
every day (apart from the fay in Pécs) to fill out the daily evaluation form, to share their ideas and also to prepare the final
outputs on the last day. Each day has a thematic and a method behind it as well as a subtopic. After every
session/workshop we will summarize the happenings, hence raise their attention about the learning key points and learning
objectives of the session and the day.

Participant with fewer opportunities
Are there participants involved in the activities who face situations that make their participation in the activities more
difficult?
Yes
Which types of situations are these participants facing?
Barriers linked to discrimination
Barriers linked to education and training systems
Cultural differences
Economic barriers
Geographical barriers
Social barriers
If any, please explain the particular measures (accompanying person, reinforced preparation etc.) you will put in place to
cater for the specific needs of these participants and/or to support their participation.
We are committed to ensuring that participants with fewer opportunities can fully engage in our project. Each partner
organization has identified at least three individuals with fewer opportunities, with some sending more. They were selected
through prior research and direct engagement by our partners, ensuring their needs and expectations are considered. They
are actively involved in project discussions, including dissemination efforts.
To address barriers linked to discrimination, we create an inclusive environment where all participants feel safe and valued.
Non-formal education methods will emphasize mutual respect and intercultural dialogue. Facilitated discussions will allow
participants to share experiences, while trained facilitators will ensure a respectful, supportive space.
For barriers linked to education and training systems, we use accessible, engaging, and adaptable activities. Participants
not in formal education will receive additional mentoring. Our experiential learning approach ensures those with limited
formal education can fully benefit. Activities will focus on practical skills, teamwork, and personal development. Support
materials will be available in accessible formats.
Cultural differences will be addressed through structured activities celebrating diversity. The project nurtures international
collaboration, and participants will share their backgrounds through storytelling, group work, and intercultural evenings. This
helps those culturally isolated in their home communities build confidence and connections.
For economic barriers, we provide financial support for travel, accommodation, and essential materials. Travel
reimbursements will be prioritized, and tickets can be purchased in advance if needed. Meals, transportation, and resources
are fully covered to ensure equitable participation.
Geographical barriers will be overcome by promoting the project online to reach participants from remote or rural areas. We
will assist with travel logistics and provide support for those who may struggle to reach the venue. This ensures a balanced
participation of urban and rural youth.
To counter social barriers, we focus on community-building activities. Some participants may struggle with integration in
their home communities, so we provide a space for them to connect with peers and form lasting relationships. Team-building
exercises, group discussions, and mentorship will enhance their engagement. They will also be invited to stay involved in
our networks post-project.
Throughout the project, participant well-being will be a shared responsibility between national team leaders, partner
organizations, and the organizing team. Team leaders will maintain direct communication with their participants, checking
on their comfort and emotional state daily. Emotional support is crucial, especially given the project’s focus on mental health
and resilience. If challenges arise, we will collaborate with sending organizations to provide immediate solutions.
Participants with fewer opportunities will be fully integrated into all activities without being singled out. They will receive the
same opportunities as others, with extra support available when needed. We support a sense of empowerment, allowing
them to challenge themselves at their own pace while feeling supported. Their involvement is not limited to
participation—they will actively contribute to project outcomes, including dissemination activities where they can share their
experiences and insights. This ensures that their voices are heard and valued, reinforcing their role as key stakeholders.
At the meeting with the team leaders we will discuss the safety protocol and from each participants we will ask to share their
necessary data for the project, here we will give space to let us know their specific needs that we will try to accommodate by
guaranteeing anonymous recognition, so they don't feel different from the other participants. Content, venue and active
participation wise we will be careful to provide equalized opportunities. Also, we will propose (if needed) several
opportunities even after program, so they can be equally entertained and participating and no social or financial barriers can
separate them by any chance. E.G. we will provide board games, snacks and (non-alcoholic) drinks in a fair way. Also
materials if they want to create something (embroidery, drawings, etc.). By this we will be support their mental strength and
involvement in the intercultural exchange as well as settle their feelings and comfort faster, so they can open up, and actively
participate/speak up also faster during the sessions.

Do you foresee Virtual/Blended activities and/or the use of any virtual component, before, during or after the activity?

Our project places a strong emphasis on environmental sustainability, ensuring that eco-friendly practices are integrated

but also carry these habits into their daily lives afterward.
One of our key commitments is eliminating the use of single-use plastics. We will not provide disposable plastic cutlery,
cups, or plates. Instead, participants will be encouraged to bring their own reusable mugs, which will be introduced in a fun
and engaging way. Before the project starts, we will ask everyone in our Facebook group to take a picture with their mug
and introduce themselves by telling a fictional story about it. This interactive activity not only boosts an early sense of
community but also strengthens the habit of using reusable items.
To reduce food waste, we will implement a mindful consumption approach. Participants will be encouraged to take only
what they need during meals, with the option to return for additional servings if necessary. By promoting the idea that food
should not be wasted, we aim to instill a more conscious attitude toward consumption. We will actively monitor food waste
and provide feedback during meal discussions to reinforce the importance of this effort.
Maintaining a clean and organized project space is another crucial aspect of our environmental efforts. Since we will be
representing our respective countries in an Erasmus+ funded initiative, we want to set a positive example wherever we go.
Participants will be responsible for their surroundings, ensuring that the project area remains tidy at all times. This includes
separating waste for recycling and supporting cleaning staff by managing their own waste responsibly. We will set up visible
and easily accessible recycling points to ensure that waste separation becomes second nature for all participants.
Energy conservation is another essential component of our environmental strategy. We will ensure that projectors, laptops,
and other electronic devices are not left plugged in when not in use. Participants will be reminded to turn off lights and
unplug chargers when their devices are fully charged. Facilitators will take the lead in setting an example, and we will
introduce a “green monitor” system where participants take turns ensuring that energy-saving practices are being followed
throughout the project.
We will also implement sustainable material use within the project activities. All educational materials will be designed for
multiple uses, minimizing unnecessary printing and single-use resources. Handouts and worksheets will be distributed
digitally whenever possible, and reusable flipcharts or whiteboards will replace traditional paper charts. Additionally, we will
ensure that all printed materials are sourced from recycled paper to further reduce environmental impact.
Our commitment to sustainability extends beyond the project itself to the way participants travel to the venue. One of our
partner organizations has agreed to prioritize green travel options, as the distance is short enough to avoid flights. We will
encourage participants to travel together in shared cars, buses, or trains to minimize their carbon footprint. Furthermore,
once in Pécs, participants will be encouraged to use the city’s efficient public transportation system instead of requesting
personal pick-ups, reinforcing the importance of eco-friendly mobility.
To raise awareness and reinforce these sustainable habits, we will integrate environmental education into our daily project
activities. Each morning, we will begin with a short reflection on sustainable actions, led by rotating participant volunteers
who will share their insights and experiences. We will also organize interactive sessions where participants can exchange
their own eco-friendly habits and ideas, inspiring each other to adopt more sustainable practices. At the end of the project,
we will hold a sustainability debriefing, where participants will reflect on what they have learned and how they plan to
incorporate these practices into their everyday lives.
